content: Seems like we might want to go back to checking the compiler version for unstable features @Gary Guo: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/153523#issuecomment-4023134530
Why won't allowing unused_features work?
We are doing that, but I'd like to have a CI pass to check if we're depending on any features that we can just plainly remove
essentially the unused_features lint is totally useless in the face of already stabilized features
(well at least for lint_reasons, haven't checked others)
I don't think there's much benefit in doing so, we can always just remove features when we bump MSRV
I once wanted to go and enable all stable features in the kernel :)
(so there won't be any conflicts when people add to that list)

Gary Guo said:

I once wanted to go and enable all stable features in the kernel :)

technically we'd have to check if they exhibit the same semantics in their unstable form
My "worry" is that we add a feature to do X, then we change how we do X, no longer requiring the feature and now we depend on a feature that we don't use
I think that's fine. They'll be eventually removed when MSRV is bumped, it's not like they'll stay there forever unnoticed.
that is too weak a promise IMO
I think that is still preferable to version based feature detection which is too fragile to me.
I dislike that as well
Kernel's try-compile based detection is fine (but might be tricky to replicate in build.rs), while version-based detection is fragile.
IMO the compiler should just not trigger the unused_feature lint on stable features that are used :shrug:
I think it's a reasonable ask, because there's already a lint being generated for it
And stable features are never used in the query system, but as part of stabilization all feature gating has been removed
oh that might be a reasonable solution, just don't trigger the unused_feature lint on stable features
If there's no one from rust side picking this up, I can look at changing the compiler later this week
don't think it's that important, I'm also pretty sure I'd be able to hack this up :)
What was the purpose of triggering the lint for "unused" features that are actually used?
For the case of triggering it on stable features in general, unused or not, it sounds to me like it was thought as something to be used by nightly-projects only?
In any case, whatever is the resolution, I agree that the lint isn't that critical one way or the other. Especially now that our list is getting very small.
By the way, thinking about it, what does the lint do under conditional compilation?
i.e. the lint could potentially make things harder for little gain (for us at least).
